chain.description: Three or more nodes forming a dependency cycle (A → B → C → A).
chain.label: Chain

mutual.description: Two nodes that directly depend on each other (A ↔ B).
mutual.label: Mutual

uses.description: Import dependency — this file imports from the other.
uses.label: uses

branches.description: Decision points: if / for / while / loop / match arm / try / && / ||. Feeds `cyclomatic`.
branches.label: Branches
branches.name: Decision points

bugs.description: Estimated delivered bugs — a rough predictor of defect density.
bugs.label: Bugs
bugs.name: Halstead bugs

cloc.description: Comment-only lines (inline comments on code lines are not counted).
cloc.label: Comments
cloc.name: Comment lines

closures.description: Number of closures defined in the unit.
closures.label: Closures
closures.name: Closures

cognitive.description:
How hard the code is for a human to follow — not just how many paths it has.
Like `cyclomatic` it adds +1 for each break in linear flow (`if`, `else`, `match`, loops, `catch`, chained `&&` / `||`), but it also adds an extra +1 for every level of nesting: an `if` inside a loop inside an `if` costs far more than three flat `if`s.
That nesting penalty is the point — deeply indented logic is what actually strains a reader, so a high `cognitive` next to a modest `cyclomatic` flags tangled, hard-to-read code.
Summed across every function in the file.
cognitive.label: Cognitive
cognitive.name: Cognitive complexity

cycle.description: Cycle kind this node participates in.
cycle.label: Cycle

cyclomatic.description:
Number of independent paths through the code — roughly the minimum number of test cases needed to cover every branch.
A function starts at 1 and gains +1 per decision point: each `if` / `else if`, every `match` / `switch` arm, every loop, and each `&&` / `||` in a condition.
Summed across every function in the file, so it grows with both size and branching — the file's total branching burden.
Counts paths only, ignoring how deeply they nest. For a readability-weighted view see `cognitive`.
cyclomatic.label: Cyclomatic
cyclomatic.name: Cyclomatic complexity

effort.description: Mental effort to implement the algorithm.
effort.label: Effort
effort.name: Halstead effort

eta1.description: Distinct operators (η₁): the count of unique operator token kinds. JavaScript / TypeScript count the keywords `export import from as extends new function let var const return delete throw break continue if else switch case default for in of while try catch finally with async await yield`, arithmetic / logical / comparison / assignment operators (`+ - * / % ** ++ -- && || ! == === != !== < <= > >= = += -= *= /= %= **= ?? ?`), bitwise operators (`& | ^ << >> >>> ~`), and punctuation / delimiters (`. , : ; ( [ { @`).
eta1.label: η₁
eta1.name: Unique operators

eta2.description: Distinct operands (η₂): the count of unique operand texts. JavaScript / TypeScript count identifiers (including `member_expression` / `property_identifier` / `nested_identifier`), literals (`string`, `number`, `true`, `false`, `null`, `undefined`, `void`), `this` / `super`, and the contextual keywords `set` / `get` / `typeof` / `instanceof`.
eta2.label: η₂
eta2.name: Unique operands

exits.description: Number of exit points (return/throw) in the unit.
exits.label: Exits
exits.name: Exit points

fan_in.description: Number of nodes that depend on this one. High fan-in means broadly reused.
fan_in.label: Fan-in
fan_in.name: Fan-in

fan_out.description: Number of nodes this one depends on. High fan-out means many dependencies. External-library edges are counted separately.
fan_out.label: Fan-out
fan_out.name: Fan-out

fan_out_external.description: Number of distinct external libraries this node depends on.
fan_out_external.label: Fan-out (external)

hk.description: Henry–Kafura — combines unit size with incoming/outgoing coupling (internal edges only).
hk.label: HK
hk.name: Henry–Kafura

length.description: Program length — total operator + operand occurrences.
length.label: Length
length.name: Halstead length

lloc.description: Logical lines — counts statements, not physical lines.
lloc.label: Logical
lloc.name: Logical lines

mi.description: Maintainability Index (0–100, higher is more maintainable). Derived from Halstead volume, cyclomatic complexity, and SLOC.
mi.label: MI
mi.name: Maintainability index

mi_sei.description: SEI variant of the Maintainability Index — adds a bonus for comment density.
mi_sei.label: MI (SEI)
mi_sei.name: Maintainability (SEI)

n1.description: Total operators (N₁): every operator occurrence counted with repetition (the η₁ tokens, not deduplicated).
n1.label: N₁
n1.name: Total operators

n2.description: Total operands (N₂): every operand occurrence counted with repetition (the η₂ texts, not deduplicated).
n2.label: N₂
n2.name: Total operands

sloc.description: Source lines of code — lines with at least one non-whitespace, non-comment character. Blank and comment-only lines are not counted (unlike `loc`, the raw file line count).
sloc.label: Source
sloc.name: Source lines

spaces.description: Unit count: the source file (1) plus each function / impl / trait / closure space. Feeds `cyclomatic`.
spaces.label: Spaces
spaces.name: Unit count

span_sloc.description: Line span of the unit (end_row − start_row) — the size input the Maintainability Index (`mi` / `mi_sei`) is computed from.
span_sloc.label: Span
span_sloc.name: Line span

volume.description: Algorithm size in bits, from distinct operators and operands.
volume.label: Volume
volume.name: Halstead volume

presets.label: CPX
presets.prompt:
These modules are too complex and I want to reduce their complexity.
Reduce it by splitting large units into smaller single-responsibility ones,
extracting repeated patterns into shared helpers, flattening deeply nested
control flow, and breaking large functions into focused helpers.
presets.title: CPX — Reduce Complexity

presets.label: ADP
presets.prompt:
The dependency graph between modules must form a DAG. When module A depends
on module B, no chain of dependencies should bring B back to A.

Identify any cycles in the modules below. For each cycle, propose a concrete
refactoring (extract a shared abstraction, invert a dependency, split a module)
that makes the graph acyclic without breaking existing functionality.

When splitting a module to break a cycle, the new structure should:
- Preserve existing API contracts
- Minimise coupling in the new structure
- Follow the Single Responsibility Principle
- Not introduce new dependency cycles
presets.title: ADP — Acyclic Dependencies Principle

presets.label: SRP
presets.prompt:
A module should have one reason to change — it should serve one actor
and encapsulate one coherent set of decisions.

For each module below, identify whether it has more than one responsibility.
Propose how to split responsibilities so each module changes for only one reason,
and specify the new module boundaries.
presets.title: SRP — Single Responsibility Principle

presets.label: OCP
presets.prompt:
A module should be open for extension but closed for modification: new behaviour
should be addable without editing existing, working code.

For each module below, identify extension points that currently require editing
existing code (e.g. growing match/switch/if-else chains). Propose an extension
mechanism (polymorphism, strategy, plug-in registration) so new cases can be added
without modifying these modules.
presets.title: OCP — Open/Closed Principle

presets.label: LSP
presets.prompt:
Every implementation of an interface must honour its full contract — return-value
invariants, error/exception behaviour, side effects, and resource ownership — not
just the method signatures. A subtype must be substitutable for its base without
surprising callers.

Identify the interface implementations in the modules below. For each, check it can
replace any other implementation of the same interface without breaking callers.
Flag violations and propose fixes.
presets.title: LSP — Liskov Substitution Principle

presets.label: ISP
presets.prompt:
Clients should not be forced to depend on methods they do not use. Prefer several
small, focused interfaces over one wide interface.

Identify interfaces in the modules below that are wider than their consumers need.
Propose how to split them into narrower interfaces so each consumer depends only on
what it actually uses.
presets.title: ISP — Interface Segregation Principle

presets.label: DIP
presets.prompt:
High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules; both should depend on
abstractions, and abstractions should not depend on details.

Find places in the modules below where a high-level module depends directly on a
concrete low-level type. Propose an abstraction (interface) to invert each such
dependency, and specify where the concrete implementation should be wired in.
presets.title: DIP — Dependency Inversion Principle

presets.label: DRY
presets.prompt:
Every piece of knowledge must have a single authoritative representation.
DRY is about knowledge duplication, not just code duplication.

Identify concepts, rules, or policies that are duplicated across the modules
below. For each duplication, propose a canonical location and the refactoring
needed to consolidate it.
presets.title: DRY — Don't Repeat Yourself

presets.label: KISS
presets.prompt:
When two designs solve the same problem, prefer the simpler one — fewer
abstractions, fewer indirection layers, fewer moving parts.

Identify over-engineered or needlessly complex constructs in the modules below.
For each, describe the simpler alternative and estimate the risk of simplifying.
presets.title: KISS — Keep It Simple

presets.label: LoD
presets.prompt:
A method should only call methods on: itself, its direct fields,
its parameters, and objects it constructs locally.
Avoid `x.foo().bar().baz()` chains that traverse object graphs.

Identify method chains or deep field traversals in the modules below that
violate LoD. For each, propose a narrow accessor or a facade that exposes only
what the caller needs, reducing coupling.
presets.title: Law of Demeter — Principle of Least Knowledge

presets.label: MISU
presets.prompt:
Move correctness from runtime checks into the type system, so invalid states
cannot be constructed and fail at compile time rather than at runtime.

Identify data structures or function signatures in the modules below where invalid
states are representable at runtime. For each, propose a type-level encoding
(sum type / enum, newtype, typestate) that makes the invalid state unrepresentable
by construction.
presets.title: MISU — Make Invalid States Unrepresentable

presets.label: CoI
presets.prompt:
Build behaviour by composing small, focused pieces rather than through deep
inheritance hierarchies.

Identify large types that accumulate behaviour in the modules below. Propose how to
decompose them into smaller composable parts, and show how consumers would assemble
the behaviour they need.
presets.title: CoI — Composition Over Inheritance

presets.label: YAGNI
presets.prompt:
Build for the problem you have now, not one you imagine you might have later.
Don't add an abstraction, a generic parameter, or a public API for a hypothetical
future use.

Identify abstractions, generics, or public APIs in the modules below that were
added speculatively. For each, assess whether multiple real callers use it today,
and propose simplification if not.
presets.title: YAGNI — You Aren't Gonna Need It

presets.label: HK
presets.prompt:
These modules carry heavy Henry-Kafura coupling — HK = sloc × (fan_in × fan_out)²,
where sloc is the module's source lines of code (real code lines, excluding blanks
and comment-only lines), fan_in is how many modules depend on it, and fan_out is how
many it depends on.
A high score is a large module sitting on a busy crossroads of incoming and outgoing
dependencies, so any change here ripples widely.

For each module below, lower the factor that dominates its HK: shrink the module by
extracting cohesive pieces, or cut fan-in/fan-out by narrowing its public surface and
depending on fewer collaborators (introduce an abstraction, move a responsibility).
Keep existing API contracts intact.
presets.title: HK — Henry-Kafura Coupling

presets.label: SLOC
presets.prompt:
These are the largest modules by source lines of code. Size alone is not a defect, but
oversized files usually bundle several responsibilities and are hard to read, test and
review.

For each module below, identify the distinct responsibilities it holds and propose how
to split it into smaller, cohesive modules — each with a single clear purpose — without
changing external behaviour.
presets.title: SLOC — Module Size

presets.label: FANIN
presets.prompt:
These modules have high fan-in: many other modules depend on them. They are
load-bearing — a change here forces changes (or re-review) across every dependant, and
a bug here is widely felt.

For each module below, confirm its public surface is a stable, minimal contract. Narrow
the API to what callers actually need, split it if different callers use disjoint parts
(see Interface Segregation), and stabilise the abstractions the rest of the codebase
leans on.
presets.title: Fan-in — Afferent Coupling

presets.label: FANOUT
presets.prompt:
These modules have high fan-out: they depend on many other modules. High efferent
coupling makes a module fragile (it breaks when any dependency changes) and hard to
test or reuse in isolation.

For each module below, reduce its direct dependencies: depend on abstractions rather
than concretes (see Dependency Inversion), collapse several fine-grained collaborators
behind one focused interface, and move logic that pulls in unrelated dependencies into
a more appropriate module.
presets.title: Fan-out — Efferent Coupling
